items.title: In the Trenches with 50 Midwest CIOs
items.summary: Of 50 enterprise CIOs in a room, zero said they're using agents at scale, and only 5/25 had any agents in production; the barrier isn't model quality but the absence of documented workflows for agents to operate on. Matters as ground-level enterprise data contradicting vendor hype and validating the 'missing context layer' thesis for enterprise AI stagnation.

items.title: Slop 'Em Up: Slop vs Taste
items.summary: The 'slop cannon' is reframed as a workflow mode—using AI output as raw material to think with, then editing hard—with production mode being fast and selection mode being slow; the bottleneck is always taste, not generation. Matters as a practitioner-level operationalization of the taste-is-the-bottleneck school with a specific two-mode workflow design.

items.title: Learning on the Shop Floor: Shopify's River Agent
items.summary: Shopify's River AI agent operates only in public Slack channels (never DMs), so employees learn from watching each other use it; 5,938 employees used River across 4,450 channels in 30 days, and it authored ~1 in 8 merged PRs, with merge rate improving from 36% to 77% through collective learning. Matters as the clearest live example of an AI-native org design principle: transparency as capability multiplier.

items.title: Escape from Agentic Loop
items.summary: David Hoang distinguishes human-on-the-loop (supervising autonomous agents) from human-in-the-loop (deeply involved in execution); taste and original work require being in the loop, while commodity tasks can safely be delegated. Matters as a practitioner taxonomy for which work should remain human—with the argument that over-delegation atrophies the judgment that made you worth delegating to.

items.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
items.summary: AI-generated content is increasingly impossible to filter and may be causing human writers to mimic AI style, homogenizing online discourse and creating a 'zombie internet' of semi-automated interactions. Matters as documented evidence of AI deployment externalities at scale—distinct from fake content, it's a homogenization effect on the collective epistemic environment.

items.title: You Need AI That Reduces Maintenance Costs
items.summary: AI coding tools increase output speed but don't automatically reduce maintenance costs, which compound exponentially without deliberate management; productivity gains don't last if you're shipping faster but accumulating harder-to-maintain code. Matters as a practitioner counter-narrative to vibe-coding optimism, with a concrete argument that coding speed and code health are separate problems.

items.title: Running Codex Safely at OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI's internal playbook for deploying Codex uses OS-enforced sandboxing, network allowlists, and AI-powered security triage that reviews Codex's own action logs; a two-phase runtime (network-on for setup, network-off for execution) limits blast radius. Matters as the first detailed public account of how a frontier lab manages its own coding agent internally, establishing a reference architecture for production agent deployment.

items.title: How to Orchestrate AI Workflows
items.summary: Luca Rossi navigates the tradeoffs between pure AI agents and deterministic orchestration, proposing a maturity model where teams start AI-first and gradually replace AI with code as workflows mature. Challenges the 'all-agents-all-the-time' narrative with a practical scaffolding-first framework.

items.title: The Fallacy of the 16-hour Agent
items.summary: Argues that the widely-cited 16-hour agent benchmark conflates duration with difficulty and is based on a 50% success rate — at 80% reliability the same model handles only ~3-hour tasks. Grounds the agent capability debate in success rates rather than headline duration numbers.

items.title: Socrates as a Service
items.summary: Argues that deep conversational questioning to extract tacit knowledge is still a distinctly human skill AI cannot replicate. The gap between AI output and a skilled human interviewer is where the best ideas still live.

items.title: Interaction Models: A Scalable Approach to Human-AI Collaboration
items.summary: Research preview of multi-stream interaction models trained from scratch for real-time audio/video/text collaboration, eliminating turn-based limits. Represents a new architectural approach that could reshape how agentic systems handle real-time human interaction.

items.title: Foundation Model Scaling: Building Blocks
items.summary: AWS details how foundation model scaling has shifted from pre-training toward post-training and test-time compute, with coverage of distributed infrastructure required at each stage. Important for understanding where frontier labs are investing compute in 2026.

items.title: The Inference Shift
items.summary: Argues inference is splitting into two regimes — low-latency answer inference (where Cerebras WSE-3 dominates with 44GB on-chip SRAM) versus agentic inference (where HBM GPUs dominate for large KV caches). Cerebras' surging IPO signals this bifurcation and its infrastructure implications for agent deployment.

items.title: Using Claude Code: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of HTML
items.summary: Thariq Shihipar of Anthropic makes the case that HTML has overtaken Markdown as the superior format for AI agent outputs — richer, interactive, shareable, and already the Anthropic internal default. Has triggered significant industry debate about the appropriate interface contract between agents and humans.

items.title: Thoughts on GitLab's Workforce Reduction and Agentic Era Restructuring
items.summary: Simon Willison analyzes GitLab restructuring around the agentic era — flattening management, creating 60 smaller teams with end-to-end ownership, pulling back from small-country offices — and notes the incentive structure that makes GitLab uniquely motivated to believe agents will grow the software market. A sharp read of a major organizational signal.

items.title: AI Is Breaking Two Vulnerability Cultures
items.summary: AI's ability to simultaneously find and fix bugs is breaking coordinated-disclosure culture and bug-bounty economics by making manual vulnerability hunting less economically viable. Signals how AI is reshaping security profession incentive structures.

items.title: AutoTTS: Agentic Test-Time Scaling
items.summary: Explores automated strategy discovery for test-time scaling using coding agents that iteratively refine controller logic in a replay environment — no gradient updates needed. Advances the frontier of agents improving their own reasoning strategies without expensive retraining.

items.title: The Main Path to Truly Creative AI
items.summary: Argues AI cannot be truly creative without intrinsic drives and subjective experience, and that emulating feelings may be the necessary path — but raises ethical concerns akin to parental responsibility. Frames the creativity gap as an architectural and values question rather than a pure scaling question.

items.title: LocalMaxxing
items.summary: Argues local models can now match cloud models for many tasks at dramatically lower cost, signaling a shift in where AI workloads run and who controls inference economics. Relevant to the debate about whether cloud AI infrastructure moats are defensible against local competition.

items.title: Mining Your Life for Context
items.summary: Dan Shipper profiles how AI entrepreneur Noah Brier uses Claude Code as a second brain, with Every head of growth Austin Tedesco on using Codex to spot interruptions and mine scattered past insights into drafts. Matters because it offers a concrete workflow for context-engineering practice: treating past outputs and conversations as retrievable structured memory.

items.title: How AI Changes Software P&L
items.summary: SaaS used to mean 70-80% gross margins; AI-native software may cap at 17% because every call is personalized, reasoning models burn 10-100x more tokens, and inference costs scale linearly with users. Four escape routes are sketched: ultra-low-cost clones, niche lifestyle businesses, luxury software, and vertical integration—with the clear message that decks still assuming 80% margins need rewriting.

items.title: Who's Winning Enterprise AI Now: Claude Up 128%, Gemini Up 48%, OpenAI Down 8%, Grok Still a Rounding Error
items.summary: Enterprise AI adoption data over the past 12 months: OpenAI still leads at 56% share but dropped 8%; Claude more than doubled; Gemini grew from 27% to 40%; coding-assistant market is driving the shift. Matters as empirical evidence of which providers are actually winning enterprise seats and why.

items.title: How to Become AI-Native
items.summary: AI-native is an organizational label, not a technology one: only ~1,000 companies above M ARR have rebuilt so agents operate inside core workflows rather than at the edges. The shift requires making implicit knowledge explicit—documented decision rules, agent-readable SOPs, clean customer objects, permissions, and evaluation loops.

items.title: GitLab Act 2
items.summary: CEO Bill Staples announces a sweeping restructuring: 30% operational footprint reduction, three layers of management removed, ~60 smaller autonomous R&D teams, and five architectural bets including a new git system for machine-scale agents. The thesis is explicit: 'Software will be built by machines, directed by people,' making this a landmark case study in a public company pivoting to an agentic platform.

items.title: Software Engineering May No Longer Be a Lifetime Career
items.summary: Using AI to code means slower skill accumulation, but refusing to use it is professionally uncompetitive—analogous to construction workers required to do body-damaging lifts. The essay argues software engineering is converging on a professional-sports model: a finite career window rather than a lifetime of compounding expertise.

items.title: Thin Harness, Fat Skills
items.summary: YC CEO Garry Tan's thesis: agent productivity lives in purpose-built skill files (fat markdown documents the agent reads and executes), not in the runtime harness. Push fuzzy judgment operations into skills; push must-be-perfect deterministic operations into code; keep the harness thin—this is the new DRY principle for agentic engineering.

items.title: Building Self-Repairing Agent Loops
items.summary: OpenAI's Codex workflow for agents that iteratively review, repair, and validate outputs using structured feedback loops to improve reliability without human intervention. Matters because it codifies a production pattern—iterative-repair-loop—that is emerging as a key primitive for reliable autonomous agents.

items.title: Reinforcing Recursive Language Models
items.summary: Using RL to fine-tune 4B-parameter models as Recursive Language Models (RLMs) achieves Claude Sonnet 4.6-level performance at significantly lower cost by training a shared policy for parent and child RLMs. Matters because it challenges the assumption that frontier-level task performance requires frontier-scale models—a direct scaling-law provocation.

items.title: Compute Optimal Tokenization
items.summary: Training ~1,300 models reveals that the standard 20-tokens-per-parameter scaling heuristic is an artifact of specific tokenizers, not a universal law; compute-optimal scaling should use bytes rather than tokens. Matters because it suggests current scaling guidance systematically underestimates compute allocation for multilingual and byte-heavy domains.

items.title: AI for the Real World: A Conversation with Yann LeCun
items.summary: LeCun argues today's LLMs are commercially valuable but won't reach human-level intelligence because language is a small fraction of how humans understand the world; future AI must use world models (JEPA architecture) to learn abstract representations of physics, causality, and consequences. LeCun recently founded AMI Labs with a B+ seed to build exactly this, making this an essential statement of the anti-LLM scaling school.

items.title: The New Agentic Search Models
items.summary: Agentic search models are specialized LLMs trained specifically on search tasks, representing a divergence from general-purpose models for retrieval-heavy agentic workflows. Matters because it signals the beginning of capability specialization within the agent stack—a shift from one-model-does-all toward purpose-built components.

items.title: Fast Mode for Claude Opus 4.7
items.summary: Fast mode for Opus 4.7—2.5x faster at 6x standard pricing (0/50 per M tokens)—is now in research preview across Claude Code, Cursor, Emergent, Factory, v0, Warp, and Windsurf; currently opt-in but will become default. Matters because it formalizes a speed/cost/quality tradeoff dial for production coding-agent workflows.

items.title: The Wu Tapes
items.summary: Long-form interview with Scott Wu, CEO of Cognition (Devin), covering his path from childhood math olympiad to building one of the fastest-growing companies in history around autonomous AI coding agents. Matters as a first-person account of the agent-native coding thesis from the person who arguably proved it in production.

items.title: Auto-Improving Software
items.summary: Bedi runs an entire agent development lifecycle through five Claude Code prompts: scaffold, harden against spec, add capabilities, fix eval failures, and reconcile drift. The 'Improve loop' derives 8-12 probes from an agent's instructions, runs them against the live container, judges PASS/FAIL, and iterates up to five rounds—a concrete pattern for self-healing production agents.

items.title: Codex Is for Prosumers — Here's Why (and How) to Switch
items.summary: a16z's Olivia Moore recommends non-technical knowledge workers migrate to OpenAI's Codex from Claude's interfaces, arguing Codex's one-click Skills and lower setup friction (vs. Claude's ~10% non-programmer setup rate) will anchor the first real prosumer agent marketplace. Matters as a concrete claim about which agentic product is winning the non-technical user.

items.title: AI versus Microservices
items.summary: Every technology shift changes the dominant architecture and languages—the essay argues AI is doing the same to microservices, suggesting LLM-friendly architectures (monolithic context windows, agent-readable service boundaries) will displace decomposed microservice graphs. Matters as a practitioner-level challenge to the prevailing microservices orthodoxy in the age of coding agents.

items.title: The Last Company
items.summary: The thesis: future businesses will compete with AI-lab-owned firms where every knowledge-intensive function has been replaced by AI, creating an inherent structural advantage that independent companies cannot match on cost. Matters because it frames the services-as-software trend not as opportunity but as existential competitive threat for traditional firms.

items.title: Google Brings Agentic AI and Vibe-Coded Widgets to Android
items.summary: Google's new Gemini-powered Android features can complete actions across apps, browse the web, fill out forms, and generate custom widgets through natural language prompts. Matters because it marks vibe-coding and agent-native browsing reaching the consumer OS layer—no longer confined to developer tools.

items.title: Introducing Claude Platform on AWS
items.summary: Claude Platform is now GA on AWS: customers access Anthropic's native Claude APIs, features, and console through an AWS account but the service is operated by Anthropic outside AWS's security boundary. Matters as a milestone in AI distribution—enterprise AI procurement increasingly flows through cloud hyperscaler billing, reshaping the buy-vs-build calculus.

items.title: Recursive Superintelligence Raises $650M to Build Self-Improving AI
items.summary: Recursive Superintelligence emerged from stealth with $650M at a $4.65B valuation, with a founding team drawn from OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI, and Salesforce, to build AI that autonomously identifies its weaknesses, designs fixes, and implements them without human intervention. The startup's bet on recursive self-improvement as a near-term engineering target marks a new category of explicitly AGI-directed labs.

items.title: The Economics of Superstar AI Researchers
items.summary: Top frontier-lab researchers now earn over 100x more than the average AI postdoc because their contributions scale instantly to billions of users, making researcher quality far more economically valuable than quantity. This superstar economics pattern explains the intensity of talent wars at frontier labs and suggests researcher compensation will continue to diverge.

items.title: Building a Safe, Effective Sandbox to Enable Codex on Windows
items.summary: OpenAI details the engineering choices behind Codex's Windows sandbox, which uses separate OS accounts and explicit network allowlists because Windows has no native primitive for a 'safe autonomous coding agent.' The writeup surfaces the real infrastructure cost of deploying coding agents in enterprise environments where security and developer-workflow compatibility both matter.

items.title: AI Gateway Production Index
items.summary: Seven months of AI gateway traffic across 200,000+ teams shows agentic workloads now carry 59% of all token volume, with Anthropic leading on spend despite higher unit prices and heavy multi-model routing becoming the production norm. The data confirms that multi-model, multi-agent architectures are no longer experimental.

items.title: Cline Releases Open-Source Agent Runtime SDK for Coding Agents
items.summary: @cline/sdk is an open-source framework for coding agents with checkpoints, MCP support, cron jobs, and native subagent orchestration, designed for CI/CD pipelines and embedded agent products. It signals the maturation of a composable infrastructure layer for coding agents independent of any single model provider.

items.title: Anthropic Beats OpenAI on Business Adoption
items.summary: More businesses used Anthropic than OpenAI in April 2026 for the first time, with Anthropic quadrupling enterprise adoption in a year while OpenAI's business adoption grew only 0.3%. This structural crossing-over overrides typical vendor stickiness and marks a genuine inversion in the enterprise AI market.

items.title: What Parameter Golf Taught Us
items.summary: OpenAI's 16MB model compression challenge attracted 1,000+ participants and 2,000 submissions, with AI coding agents lowering experimentation costs enough to uncover new techniques like quantization-aware training from non-expert participants. The competition doubles as a talent pipeline and reveals how open challenges accelerate research at the efficiency frontier.

items.title: Apple Is Working to Incorporate AI Agents on the App Store
items.summary: Apple is exploring how to let AI agents into the App Store while preserving its privacy and security policies, which conflict with agents' need to take complex cross-app actions autonomously. This represents the platform tension between Apple's walled-garden revenue model and the emerging agent-first distribution paradigm.

items.title: Is Software Losing Its Head?
items.summary: Seema Amble examines what remains valuable in enterprise software when agents bypass the UI layer, arguing incumbents betting on the data layer as their moat must also own the identity and permissioning layer for agent-to-agent interactions to stay relevant. The piece maps three strategic responses for SaaS companies facing headless displacement.

items.title: The State of Product Development — with Doug Peete
items.summary: Luca Rossi and Atono CPO Doug Peete discuss findings from an industry report on product development, covering why planning is fragile, the requirements problem, and AI for product specs as an underexploited opportunity where most teams haven't applied AI. A practitioner lens on where AI-assisted product processes break down and what must be fixed before AI can help.

items.title: Opus 4.7 Reels Us Back In
items.summary: Every's Context Window tests Claude Opus 4.7 through editorial and engineering tasks, finding it proactively suggests optimizations and performs like a senior magazine editor, pulling the team back after experimenting with other models. The review includes how Every defines 'agent' and a documented security breach (the Mini Shai-Hulud), offering a calibrated practitioner assessment of the model.

items.title: Defense at AI Speed: Microsoft's MDASH Multi-Model Agentic Security System
items.summary: MDASH orchestrates 100+ specialized AI agents across frontier and distilled models to find, debate, and prove real-world software vulnerabilities, reaching 88.45% on the CyberGym benchmark and discovering 16 new Windows flaws including 4 critical RCEs. It demonstrates that multi-agent deliberation loops can exceed single-model performance on complex real-world security tasks.

items.title: How We Built Security Into Computer
items.summary: Perplexity details the Firecracker microVM isolation, scoped connector permissions, and four-layer prompt injection defense powering their Computer autonomous agent, with each session running in its own Linux kernel and a dedicated browser-safety ML classifier scanning external content. A valuable reference architecture for production autonomous agents that browse the web.

items.title: Amazon Ditches Rufus, Launches Alexa Shopping Agent
items.summary: Amazon is making Alexa the centerpiece of its AI shopping strategy, replacing the standalone Rufus chatbot with an agentic shopping assistant that can take purchasing actions using personal history and preferences. The pivot from chatbot to autonomous shopping agent signals Amazon's commitment to the services-as-software model for e-commerce.

items.title: AI Is the New Netflix
items.summary: Om Malik argues AI is reshaping internet traffic as Netflix did, now driving massive upstream bandwidth demand that breaks the old asymmetric broadband model built for passive consumption. AI use is creating upstream traffic at scale that didn't exist a few years ago, permanently ending the fat-downstream/thin-upstream broadband design.

items.title: Adaption AutoScientist: An AI Tool That Helps Models Train Themselves
items.summary: Adaption's AutoScientist automates the fine-tuning loop—generating hypotheses, running experiments, and iterating—so models can rapidly acquire specific capabilities with minimal human intervention. An early data point in the shift toward self-directed training pipelines where humans set goals rather than execute experiments.

items.title: We Gave Every Employee an AI Agent. Here's What We're Doing Differently Now.
items.summary: Every ran a two-month experiment giving every employee an OpenClaw agent, then redesigned after finding individual agents created more friction than leverage; the revised model treats agents as shared team resources with defined jobs, not personal AI pets. A firsthand org-design data point that directly challenges the naive one-agent-per-employee assumption.

items.title: Introducing Grok Build Early Beta
items.summary: xAI launches Grok Build, a terminal-native agentic coding CLI with parallel subagent support, worktree integrations, MCP server compatibility, and a headless mode for CI/CD automation—available to SuperGrok Heavy subscribers. Adds competitive pressure to the Claude Code/Codex duopoly and signals that all major frontier labs now have a terminal coding agent.

items.title: Development Environments for Your Cloud Agents
items.summary: Cursor details a cloud-based development environment system for autonomous coding agents: multi-repo context, Dockerfile-based configuration, automated setup workflows, and governance controls for managing fleets of parallel agents. Represents the infrastructure layer needed to graduate from one coding agent to a governed fleet running simultaneously.

items.title: 2028: Two Scenarios for Global AI Leadership
items.summary: Anthropic presents two 2028 scenarios—US democratic leadership maintained through export controls versus authoritarian norm-setting if policy inaction lets China close the gap—arguing 2026 decisions are the hinge point for who governs AI globally. The most explicit AGI-timeline-meets-governance argument published by a frontier lab to date.

items.title: From "System of Record" to "System of Intelligence"
items.summary: a16z argues CRM and systems of record are evolving into AI orchestration layers that drive intelligent action across business functions, with winners owning the system-of-intelligence tier that combines proprietary data with agent orchestration. Frames the SaaS-to-AI-services transition as a race between incumbents rebundling as intelligence layers and AI-native upstarts.

items.title: When Knowledge Is Cheap, Insight Is Everything
items.summary: Applies Jevons Paradox to AI: as the cost of finding and synthesizing information collapses, demand for that information explodes and the bottleneck shifts from access to genuine insight and taste. Provides the economic framework for why AI amplifies people who already have strong judgment rather than leveling the playing field.

items.title: Recursive Superintelligence Raises $650M to Build Self-Improving AI
items.summary: Recursive Superintelligence, backed by GV and Nvidia at a $4.65B valuation, is building AI systems that can recursively improve themselves with minimal human oversight; its seven co-founders come from OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI, and Salesforce AI. A major capital bet on self-improving AI that places AGI-timeline and alignment debates squarely in the investment mainstream.

items.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
items.summary: Willison surfaces the "Zombie Internet" concept—a web where AI talks to AI, humans use AI to talk to other humans using AI, and authenticity signals collapse into noise—reflecting on the second-order social costs of mass AI adoption. A sharp practitioner essay on the hidden costs of AI ubiquity and the erosion of trust in online content.

items.title: Announcing Genkit Middleware: Intercept, Extend, and Harden Your Agentic Apps
items.summary: Google's Genkit framework now includes a composable middleware system for agentic apps that intercepts generation calls to enable retries, human-approval gates before destructive tool calls, and full-stack observability—supporting TypeScript, Go, Dart, and Python. A concrete reference implementation of production-grade agent hardening patterns for teams moving past prototype agents.

items.title: Claude for Small Business
items.summary: Anthropic launches a pre-built package of Claude connectors and workflows integrating with QuickBooks, PayPal, HubSpot, Google Workspace, and Microsoft 365 targeting SMBs that want AI without custom development. Signals the shift from AI-as-API toward AI-as-packaged-service, compressing the adoption curve for non-technical businesses.

items.title: How We Built Secure, Scalable Agent Sandbox Infrastructure
items.summary: Two patterns for sandboxing agents that can execute code: isolate the tool (sandboxed subprocess) or isolate the entire agent (stateless container with zero secrets, a control plane holding all credentials). The isolate-the-agent approach removes anything worth stealing and enables independent kill-restart-scale cycles, at the cost of an extra network hop per operation.

items.title: Unlocking Asynchronicity in Continuous Batching
items.summary: Using CUDA streams and events to overlap CPU batch-preparation with GPU computation, this technique cuts idle time between cycles and improves GPU utilisation for inference by 22% without changing kernels or model weights. As inference compute grows to dominate AI infrastructure costs, eliminating idle GPU cycles becomes a significant efficiency lever.

items.title: Microsoft Is Quietly Shopping for an OpenAI Replacement
items.summary: Microsoft amended its OpenAI exclusivity deal (freeing OpenAI to sell on any cloud, removing the AGI clause) while simultaneously exploring acquisitions of AI startups including Inception, which builds diffusion-based LLMs, to reduce dependency ahead of its 2032 IP license expiry. The move signals that big-tech AI partnerships are shifting from exclusive lock-in to hedged multi-vendor strategies.

items.title: Claude Code's /goals Separates the Agent That Works from the One That Decides It's Done
items.summary: /goal is emerging as a universal primitive across coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Hermes): a small validator model runs after every agent step and checks whether the done condition is met, closing the loop without user approval. Every major AI lab shipped this same primitive within six weeks, signalling industry convergence on a shared interface for closed-loop autonomous coding.

items.title: Apple-OpenAI Alliance Frays, Setting Up Possible Legal Fight
items.summary: OpenAI is exploring legal options against Apple over underdelivered ChatGPT integration depth and subscriber growth, while Apple prepares to open Siri to rival providers (Anthropic, Google) with iOS 27 in June. The fracture illustrates how AI distribution deals are failing when model companies expected platform amplification but received marginal embedding.

items.title: AI-Assisted Hack Bypasses Apple's Five-Year Memory Safety Defense in Five Days
items.summary: Security researchers used Anthropic's unreleased Mythos model to chain two macOS bugs into a kernel privilege escalation exploit bypassing Apple's Memory Integrity Enforcement on M5 hardware, built in roughly five days. Anthropic's response, Project Glasswing (controlled defensive access for Apple, Microsoft, Google), signals a new model where frontier AI is too capable for unrestricted release and must be managed as a dual-use research tool.

items.title: From System of Record to System of Intelligence
items.summary: AI agents are transforming CRMs and GTM software from passive databases into autonomous orchestration layers that research, coach, and log notes without expanding headcount. The new moat is owning a system of intelligence, the full data model and workflow layer an agent needs to operate, not the human-facing dashboard that previously locked customers in.

items.title: The Shape of the Thing
items.summary: Mollick marks late 2025 as the inflection into a new era of agentic AI where you delegate work and get results back in minutes — moving from co-intelligence to managing AIs; illustrates with the StrongDM three-person Software Factory that ships production code without human involvement. Essential framing of the manager-of-agents paradigm from a leading AI-in-work researcher.

items.title: Management as AI Superpower
items.summary: Mollick argues that traditional management skills — goal-setting, delegation, review, feedback — are the key leverage point for getting value from AI agents, documented through an MBA class where students built startups in four days using agents. Reframes management competence as the core bottleneck in the agentic era.

items.title: From Vibe Coding to Spec-Driven Development
items.summary: Documents the professional engineering shift from vibe coding (natural-language prompting) to spec-driven development — structured, version-controlled markdown specs that serve as the single source of truth for coding agents; GitHub Spec Kit and AWS Kiro report up to 10x fewer regeneration cycles. Maps directly to the spec-driven school and tracks the maturing of the coding-agent workflow.

items.title: From Vibes to Engineering: How AI Agents Outgrew Their Own Terminology
items.summary: Traces the terminological and practical shift from vibe coding to agentic engineering: the real differentiator is now harness quality — the system of tools, context management, evaluation, and observability around the model — with cognitive debt emerging as the main threat. Useful synthesis of where the field actually is in mid-2026.

items.title: GitLab Announces Layoffs and Restructuring for 'Agentic Era' as AI Reshapes Developer Tools Economics
items.summary: GitLab cuts up to 30% of positions in some markets, removes three management layers, and reorganizes R&D into ~60 smaller empowered teams — explicitly framing the restructuring as preparation for an agentic era where AI agents handle coding, deployment, and maintenance. Live case study of ai-native-flat org design in action.

items.title: 2026's Real SaaS Threat Isn't AI. It's Business Model Debt.
items.summary: Argues the existential SaaS crisis is outdated per-seat billing: agentic AI collapses seat counts even as usage rises, forcing a pivot to outcome- or compute-based pricing; vendors who internalize AI efficiencies can undercut competitors with flat pricing while capturing more margin. Strong economic framing of the services-as-software and SaaS-survival debate.

items.title: Beyond the Forward Pass: How Inference-Time Scaling and World Models Are Redefining SOTA in 2026
items.summary: Frames inference-time compute — letting models think longer per answer rather than training bigger — as the dominant 2026 paradigm, projecting inference will be 75% of total AI compute by 2030; also covers world models challenging LLM dominance for embodied tasks and the failure mode of confident-but-wrong reasoning traces. Directly covers the reasoning-RL and scaling-laws-revisionism schools.

items.title: AutoResearch: AI Agents Running ML Research Experiments Automatically
items.summary: Karpathy releases an open-source autonomous AI research loop: an agent with a real LLM training setup runs ~100 experiments overnight, keeping only improvements; reached 66k+ GitHub stars and demonstrated a 19% performance gain at Shopify. The most concrete demonstration of self-improving AI agents reaching practical research use.

items.title: Not So Locked In Any More
items.summary: Willison argues that programming language choice is no longer a strong lock-in factor because coding agents can translate, rewrite, and bridge across languages at low cost — dissolving one of the oldest architectural constraints in software engineering. Relevant to coding-paradigms and the broader thesis that AI is unbundling legacy constraints.

items.title: Artificial Neurodivergence Could Help Solve the AI Alignment Problem
items.summary: Research by Hernández-Espinosa et al. proposes that instead of enforcing perfect AI obedience, we should design diverse AI agents with distinct reasoning styles and ethical priorities that can balance and correct each other — a polycentric alignment ecosystem. Novel angle on alignment that challenges single-spec approaches.

items.title: How to Start a Career When AI Is Doing Your Entry-level Job
items.summary: Examines how AI is eliminating entry-level roles as a new generation enters the workforce, with Stanford data showing 13% employment drop for young workers in AI-exposed jobs since late 2022. Argues the scarce skill is now AI judgment and evaluation rather than just prompting, and offers practical strategies for building a career when AI completes your training reps.

items.title: After the Personal Agent
items.summary: Roundup covering the pivot after personal AI agents proved too fragile to maintain, toward shared stable coworkers, plus tacit knowledge vs search, Opus 4.7 pulling users back, the 16-hour agent benchmark controversy, and mining scattered context before drafting. Documents a real early failure mode in agentic deployment and the design response.

items.title: Google Rolls Out Thinking Level Option for Gemini App
items.summary: Google is surfacing a user-adjustable Thinking level slider in the Gemini app, letting users control how much extended reasoning compute the model applies. Marks a shift toward exposing inference-time compute as a visible, user-selectable parameter relevant to reasoning-RL models.

items.title: OpenAI Will Let Codex Control Other Desktop Devices via Computer Use
items.summary: OpenAI is extending Codex beyond browser sandboxes to allow computer-use control over desktop applications and other devices. Signals the next phase of agentic coding tools escaping isolated environments and operating across the full desktop surface.

items.title: Anthropic Cache Tokenomics
items.summary: Deep-dives into the economics of Anthropic prompt caching, showing how the cache pricing structure changes cost models for large-context workloads and rewards stateful long-running applications. Argues caching is a new pricing primitive reshaping how AI infrastructure is bought and billed. candidate-school: compute-as-currency — Caching and tokenomics are creating a new pricing primitive where stateful high-context AI apps have dramatically different unit economics than stateless API calls.

items.title: How Claude Code Works in Large Codebases: Best Practices and Where to Start
items.summary: Official Anthropic guide for deploying Claude Code at enterprise scale, covering context management, subagent delegation, CLAUDE.md patterns, and repo navigation strategies. Key practitioner reference as agentic coding enters large-org adoption and the compound-engineering pattern matures.

items.title: Notes on Pretraining Parallelisms
items.summary: Technical explainer on how frontier LLM pretraining distributes computation across data, tensor, pipeline, and sequence parallelism, and why these engineering constraints shape what models are feasible to train at scale. Relevant to understanding infrastructure limits behind scaling law debates.

items.title: Recent Developments in LLM Architectures
items.summary: Surveys latest architectural innovations across large language models including mixture-of-experts routing, state-space models, attention efficiency improvements, and hybrid designs. Authoritative reference for understanding design choices shaping current and next-generation frontier models.

items.title: Lighthouse Attention: 1.4-1.7x Long-Context Pretraining Speedup
items.summary: Nous Research releases Lighthouse Attention, a selection-based hierarchical attention mechanism delivering 1.4-1.7x pretraining wall-clock speedup at 98K context and ~17x faster than standard attention at 512K context, without custom CUDA kernels. Concrete efficiency advance extending practical long-context training independent of raw scale.

items.title: The Haves and Have-Nots of the AI Gold Rush
items.summary: Examines how the AI boom is widening the competitive gap rather than leveling it, with large enterprises capturing most productivity gains while smaller companies struggle to get past pilots. Adds empirical grounding to the bubble vs not-a-bubble debate about whether AI benefits are broadly distributed.

items.title: Steering Vectors
items.summary: Technical explainer on steering vectors, activation-space interventions that redirect model behavior without fine-tuning by adding or subtracting learned direction vectors in the residual stream. Key entry point to activation engineering and its implications for interpretability and model control.

items.title: How I Use LLMs in 2026
items.summary: A software engineer first-person account of how actual day-to-day LLM workflows have evolved since 2022, what patterns stuck, what failed, and how the tool-use relationship has changed as models improved. Valuable practitioner perspective for workflow-recipes and how-to-use-ai schools.

items.title: The Sigmoids Won't Save You
items.summary: Scott Alexander argues the common assumption that AI capability growth will naturally flatten into a sigmoid is historically unfounded, with examples from solar adoption and population trends showing humans systematically predict plateaus that do not arrive on schedule. Essential reading for the AGI-timelines and extrapolators debate.

items.title: Agent Hooks: Deterministic Control
items.summary: Explains how Claude Code PreToolUse and PostToolUse hooks provide deterministic guardrails, inspecting and blocking tool calls before they execute to wrap non-deterministic AI with safety guarantees suitable for production. Key for agent-internals and spec-driven schools as lifecycle-hook patterns become standard in agentic engineering.

items.title: Domain Knowledge Is the Leverage
items.summary: Argues that as AI agents produce reasonable code faster than programmers can refactor it, the scarce and valuable skill shifts to deep domain understanding — knowing what the software should do and why it connects to the business. Maps directly to the ai-as-intensifier thesis that AI raises the value of human judgment rather than replacing it.

items.title: Clouded Judgement 5.14.26: The Real App Store Opportunity
items.summary: Jamin Ball argues that Anthropic skills, markdown files giving Claude company-specific workflow context, represent a B2B app-store moment with product teams who ship skills gaining durable distribution advantages. Strong thesis that bundling skills with software products will reshape SaaS the way app-store integration reshaped mobile.

items.title: Building AI Agents in Accounting
items.summary: Practitioner guide to deploying AI agents in accounting workflows including AP automation, reconciliation, and variance analysis, with honest assessment of what works today versus what still requires human review. Documents the services-as-software transition reaching the CFO function.

items.title: Products Are Out, Brains Are In
items.summary: Argues software company valuations are shifting from product-led metrics like seats and MAUs to intelligence-led metrics including reasoning quality, task completion rate, and decision accuracy as AI commoditizes the product layer. Frames the ai-rebundling and services-as-software transformation through an investor lens.

items.title: The AI Subscription Time Bomb
items.summary: Warns that enterprises are stacking AI subscription commitments faster than they are realizing ROI, creating a future cancellation wave when multi-year contracts come up for renewal. Relevant to the bubble-skeptics school and the saas-survives debate about whether AI tooling economics are sustainable at current pricing.

items.title: Here Comes Forward Deployed Everybody
items.summary: Analyzes the 800% YoY surge in forward-deployed engineer hiring, engineers embedded in customer sites to implement and customize AI systems, and what this signals about the broader services-as-software transition. Maps the compound-engineering-org pattern emerging as AI vendors deploy alongside their software.

items.title: Intercom, Now Called Fin, Launches an AI Agent Whose Only Job Is Managing Another AI Agent
items.summary: Intercom, rebranded as Fin, launches Fin Operator, an AI agent that manages, configures, and improves their customer-facing AI agent rather than replacing human support staff. First major commercial deployment of the manager-of-agents pattern at scale, with back-office AI overseeing front-office AI.

items.title: How to Avoid AI Code Slop
items.summary: Practitioner guide for engineering leaders on maintaining code quality when AI generates the majority of production code, covering review frameworks, context management to reduce hallucination, and when to override or reject AI output. Directly addresses the vibe-coding failure mode and the productive-friction argument.

items.title: The Race to Own the Agentic Future
items.summary: Investor thesis arguing the most defensible position in the AI stack is the agent layer, with winners determined by workflow ownership and task-completion reliability rather than model capability alone. Lays out the investment case for the services-as-software and autonomous-agent schools.

items.title: Intelligence Company
items.summary: Argues fintech companies are evolving from software providers into intelligence companies, selling financial decisions, recommendations, and autonomous actions rather than workflow tools. Strong thesis on the services-as-software transformation reaching financial services.

items.title: The Last Six Months in LLMs in Five Minutes
items.summary: Lightning-talk slides from PyCon US 2026 compressing six months of LLM developments: the best-model crown changed hands five times between three providers, coding crossed key capability thresholds, and the November 2025 inflection point redefined practitioner expectations. Authoritative practitioner survey from one of the most consistent AI commentators.

items.title: AEO, GEO vs SEO: Google AI Optimization
items.summary: Explains how AI search (Answer Engine Optimization and Generative Engine Optimization) is displacing traditional SEO as ~31% of US internet users shift to AI-generated answers in 2026. Practical guide to optimization strategies for visibility in AI-mediated discovery.

items.title: Inside the 100-agent Software Factory
items.summary: Mike Taylor tours Gas City, a project running ~100 agents that merge 50 PRs/day while burning 1B tokens/day. Introduces the 'dark factory vs light factory' split and the 'one pet, many cattle' mayor+polecats pattern for sustainable multi-agent orchestration. Matters because it's the most concrete published account of a real production multi-agent engineering system and its cost/coordination tradeoffs.

items.title: Introducing Portent: An Open Spec for Knowledge Bases
items.summary: Luca Rossi publishes Portent, an open specification for structuring AI-navigable knowledge bases for work and life, complementing his Tolaria macOS app. Matters because it proposes a standard for how agents and humans co-author and traverse markdown vaults, pushing toward folder-as-agent and spec-driven memory architectures.

items.title: Karpathy Joins Anthropic Pre-Training Team
items.summary: Andrej Karpathy joins Anthropic to build a team using Claude to accelerate pre-training research — explicitly a bet that AI-assisted research beats raw compute as the competitive lever. Matters because it signals Anthropic's thesis that recursive self-improvement at the training loop level is the next frontier.

items.title: Meta Moves 7,000 Employees Into Four AI-Native Organizations
items.summary: Meta reassigns 7K workers to Applied AI Engineering, Agent Transformation Accelerator, and Central Analytics — explicitly using 'AI-native design principles' with flatter hierarchies, smaller pods, and fewer managers per employee. Running alongside 8K layoffs, it's the clearest signal yet that a major tech org is restructuring around agent-native principles rather than adding AI to existing structure.

items.title: Anthropic Acquires Stainless for $300M+
items.summary: Anthropic acquired Stainless (SDK generator behind every official Anthropic API library, also used by OpenAI, Google, and Cloudflare) and is shutting down its hosted product. This removes a shared infrastructure supplier from competitors and brings SDK generation in-house. Matters as a compound-engineering move: controlling the developer-facing toolchain layer is a distribution and stickiness play, not just a technical one.

items.title: Introducing Cursor Composer 2.5
items.summary: Cursor's new agentic coding model matches Claude Opus 4.7 on SWE-Bench Multilingual at one-tenth the token cost, trained on 25x more synthetic tasks with RL scaling. Built with SpaceXAI and a 10x-compute successor in progress. Matters because it shows a coding-first lab closing the capability gap with frontier models while dramatically undercutting on price.

items.title: Agent Evals: How to Measure What Agents Actually Do
items.summary: Framework for evaluating AI agents beyond chat benchmarks — agents require distinct metrics for tool use, multi-step reasoning, error recovery, and goal completion. Matters because eval infrastructure is the next bottleneck for production agents and this is one of the clearer practitioner frameworks published.

items.title: Introducing Skills: Teaching Agents to Bridge Their Knowledge Gaps
items.summary: Lovable launches Skills — markdown files that teach agents how to perform domain-specific tasks, writable by anyone in an afternoon. This operationalizes the pattern that skills are modular, reusable instructions that transfer expertise into agent behavior. Matters because it makes the 'write a skill' workflow accessible to non-engineers, accelerating company-specific agent customization.

items.title: The Workflow Collision: How AI Agent Lifecycles Clash with Team Workflows
items.summary: Human team Kanban workflows (pull-based, WIP-limited, informal) collide with AI agent lifecycles (enforced state machines, upfront planning, adversarial review gates) — most teams haven't noticed yet. Matters because it surfaces a concrete integration friction point that will slow agent adoption inside existing orgs.

items.title: Manus Now Supports Scheduled Agents
items.summary: Manus adds scheduled execution — agents can now be set to run at daily, weekly, monthly, or custom intervals, automating recurring multi-step tasks with automatic output delivery. Matters as a product milestone toward ambient autonomous agents that run without a human trigger.

items.title: It's Time to Science
items.summary: Argues that 2026 in AI for Science parallels 2025 in AI for Software Engineering — the field has matured enough for AI-native research tools to matter. Both OpenAI and Anthropic have launched dedicated AI for Science divisions, signalling a structural rather than experimental shift.

items.title: Compound Engineering: How Every Codes With Agents
items.summary: Every's practical 4-step compound engineering methodology (Plan→Execute→Review→Compound) for teams building with agents, arguing that making environments agent-native is the prerequisite for velocity gains. First detailed practitioner writeup of compound engineering in production.

items.title: Inside Anthropic's 2026 Developer Conference
items.summary: Deep recap of Code with Claude 2026 featuring interviews with Anthropic product leads, covering the Managed Agents platform, SpaceX Colossus deal, multiagent orchestration, and Outcomes/Dreaming features. Essential companion to the event for practitioners building with Claude.

items.title: To Thrive in the AI Era, Companies Need Agent Managers
items.summary: HBR introduces the "agent manager" as the emerging leadership role in AI-native enterprises — responsible for orchestrating how AI agents learn, perform, and collaborate alongside humans. Argues real productivity gains require redesigning organizations around agent-first data and APIs, not merely layering AI onto human-centered systems.

items.title: 100 things we announced at Google I/O 2026
items.summary: Google I/O 2026 launches Gemini 3.5 Flash (beating Gemini 3.1 Pro on coding/agentic benchmarks at 4× speed), Gemini Spark (24/7 personal AI agent), Gemini Omni (unified multimodal model), and Antigravity 2.0 agentic dev platform. Marks Google's structural shift to agent-first positioning across Search, Gmail, and Docs.

items.title: The AI Bubble That Wasn't: How Agents Changed the Revenue Story
items.summary: Traces why the AI bubble narrative collapsed in early 2026 — the answer is agents generating measurable enterprise revenue. Mollick is quoted explaining how agentic workflows shifted the calculus from impressive demos to ROI-positive services, and why infrastructure investment is now undershooting rather than over-shooting demand.

items.title: Putting the Value Back in RL: Better Test-Time Scaling by Unifying LLM Reasoners With Verifiers
items.summary: Proposes RL^V, which jointly trains LLMs as both reasoners and generative verifiers, enabling 1.2–1.6× better test-time compute scaling vs. standard RL methods. Addresses the structural problem that GRPO and similar approaches abandon the value function needed for efficient inference-time scaling.

items.title: Beyond Scaling: Agents Are Heading to the Edge
items.summary: Argues that as LLM scaling laws plateau, the next frontier is edge-native agents — LLMs running locally in real-time volatile environments without cloud connectivity. Proposes a framework for budget-aware decentralized multi-agent swarms and examines what agency means when latency constraints eliminate cloud round-trips.

items.title: Top 10 LLM Research Papers of 2026
items.summary: Roundup of the most-upvoted 2026 LLM papers from Hugging Face, covering continuous latent diffusion models, exploratory sampling for semantic diversity, and AI-assisted long-horizon mathematical research. Useful mid-year survey of where formal LLM research is pointing.

items.title: After Automation
items.summary: Dan Shipper argues from inside Every that automation creates more human work, not less: cheap AI competence floods in and produces sameness, forcing humans to continually establish higher-order frames ahead of the models. Essential practitioner essay on why the AI-eliminates-work narrative misunderstands technological progress.

items.title: Google I/O: Agents, Agents, Agents
items.summary: Every's deep-dive on Google I/O 2026 maps Gemini 3.5 Flash and Google's new agent suite into a 'collaborate with' vs 'delegate to' taxonomy, and unpacks Anthropic's $300M Stainless acquisition as a bet on owning agent connectivity plumbing. Important for understanding how major labs compete on infrastructure layers, not just model capability.

items.title: Anthropic on Pace for First Profitable Quarter as Revenue Surges to $10.9B
items.summary: Anthropic projects $10.9B in Q2 revenue—more than doubling Q1 $4.8B—with an estimated $559M operating profit, growing faster than Google or Facebook pre-IPO. The explosive 80x growth validates the services-as-software thesis but exposes structural dependence on massive compute costs that threaten sustained profitability.

items.title: Cheap AI Could Derail OpenAI and Anthropic's IPOs
items.summary: Chinese models grew from 1% to 60% of OpenAI Router traffic while costing 9x less than Claude, threatening the premium pricing underpinning frontier lab IPO valuations. Argues that as AI commoditizes, willingness-to-pay for the last 5% of capability is far smaller than for the first 80%, collapsing enterprise pricing power.

items.title: An OpenAI Model Has Disproved a Central Conjecture in Discrete Geometry
items.summary: An OpenAI reasoning model autonomously disproved the 80-year-old Erdos planar unit distance conjecture, producing a 125-page proof using deep algebraic number theory verified by Fields Medalist Tim Gowers. The first AI resolution of a prominent open mathematics problem marks a qualitative threshold for AI-as-scientific-collaborator.

items.title: Introducing Agent Executor: Google's Distributed Agent Runtime
items.summary: Google open-sources Agent Executor, a runtime standard for durable long-running agent workflows with session consistency, trajectory branching, secure isolation, and Kubernetes-backed Agent Substrate. Signals the field converging on production infrastructure standards for agent deployment beyond one-shot inference.

items.title: With Gemini 3.5 Flash, Google Bets Its Next AI Wave on Agents, Not Chatbots
items.summary: Gemini 3.5 Flash launches GA on announcement day at 4x speed and half the cost of comparable models, powering the Spark personal agent and Antigravity developer platform. Google's all-in agentic pivot at I/O 2026 marks chatbot-first AI products as a transitional phase.

items.title: Schmoozing Is Dead: Agents Are Hitting 120% of Humans at SaaStr AI Annual 2026
items.summary: SaaStr AI Annual 2026 recap: agents already outperform human SDRs at 120%, Marc Benioff publicly concedes agents broke the enterprise sales motion, and Series A success now requires Replit-style $2M to $500M growth. Practitioners confirm services-as-software is happening, not just being theorized.

items.title: Anthropic to Pay SpaceX Nearly $45 Billion for Computing Deal
items.summary: Anthropic commits $1.25B/month through May 2029 to SpaceX for 300MW and 220K NVIDIA GPUs, revealed via the SpaceX IPO filing. Illustrates how frontier labs are now securing compute on infrastructure timescales rather than cloud pay-as-you-go, creating a new class of decade-scale capital obligation.

items.title: A Bitter Lesson for Data Filtering
items.summary: New scaling studies show no data filtering is optimal for large-model pretraining when compute is abundant: large models benefit from low-quality data rather than filtered-out data. Directly challenges prevailing data curation dogma with significant implications for pretraining pipeline design.

items.title: Anthropic's Profitability Swindle
items.summary: Ed Zitron argues Anthropic's claimed Q2 operating profit is an accounting artifact: the $1.25B/month SpaceX deal starts at a reduced rate in May/June, artificially depressing the exact expenses used to declare profitability. Also surfaces a discrepancy between sworn court filings ($5B lifetime revenue) and public projections ($4.8B in Q1 alone).

items.title: Notes From the Foothills of the Singularity
items.summary: Alex Duffy reports from Google I/O 2026, where DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis described the current moment as the 'foothills of the singularity' and put AGI 'just a few years' out, with total impact at 10x the Industrial Revolution arriving 10x faster. Matters because it shows a major lab leader publicly framing an AGI arrival window and linking public trust as the key remaining hurdle.

items.title: Lessons Learned from Building Cloud Agents
items.summary: Cursor's engineering team shares hard-won lessons from building cloud-hosted coding agents, covering reliability patterns, state management, and the architectural tradeoffs that differ from local agent execution. Directly relevant as one of the most detailed practitioner accounts of production agent infrastructure from a team at $3B ARR.

items.title: AI's Plummeting Prices Are a Software Story, Not a Hardware One
items.summary: Argues that the dramatic fall in AI inference costs is primarily driven by software innovations — model architectures, quantization, batching, and distillation — not hardware improvements. Matters because it challenges the assumption that compute cost curves are hardware-bound, shifting the locus of competitive advantage.

items.title: Frontier Labs Don't Use Most AI Compute (Yet)
items.summary: Epoch AI analysis finds that major frontier AI labs are actually underutilizing their massive compute buildouts, with training accounting for only ~40% of ML compute and inference and idle capacity taking the rest. Critical for understanding the gap between compute investment headlines and actual utilization, which complicates scaling-law projections.

items.title: Can SAEs Capture Neural Geometry?
items.summary: Goodfire research investigates whether sparse autoencoders (SAEs) — a key interpretability tool — can faithfully represent the geometric structure of neural network activations, finding significant gaps in current approaches. Matters because it directly tests a core assumption of the mechanistic interpretability research agenda.

items.title: State of AI 2026
items.summary: The 2026 annual State of Web Dev AI report studies how AI tools are reshaping developer work, adoption patterns, and productivity across the industry. A primary data source for understanding how the developer community is actually integrating AI versus how it is perceived in discourse.

items.title: Gen Z Is Not Booing AI. It Is Booing Its Own Job Market
items.summary: Reframes the wave of Gen Z graduates booing AI at commencement speeches: the anger is less about AI itself and more about entering a labor market where AI has compressed entry-level opportunity faster than new roles have appeared. Relevant as a cultural signal of the ai-economy backlash forming outside tech circles.

items.title: Introducing Agent Executor: Google's Distributed Agent Runtime
items.summary: Google open-sources Agent Executor (AX), a distributed agent runtime providing durable execution, state recovery, secure sandboxing, session consistency, and trajectory branching for long-running agentic workflows. Essential infrastructure-layer release that sets the baseline for what production agent runtimes look like at Google scale.

items.title: The Unsustainable Subsidy
items.summary: Tunguz analyzes how AI model pricing strategies across OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are shifting from subsidized growth plays to margin-conscious pricing ahead of IPOs, arguing the era of artificially cheap AI tokens is ending. Directly maps the business-model transition that determines whether current AI-native companies are building on sustainable economics.

items.title: Better Experiments with LLM Evals — A Funnel, Not a Fork
items.summary: Spotify's ML team argues that LLM evaluation should be structured as a narrowing funnel — broad checks, then targeted evals, then human review — rather than branching forks, with detailed guidance on implementing this in production. Valuable practitioner essay on the emerging discipline of AI product quality assurance.

items.title: Coding Is Solved? Software Is Not
items.summary: Even as AI agents can generate credible code in minutes, the actual challenge in software development has shifted to judgment — deciding what to build, whether the right problem was solved, and whether the change was the correct one to make. A sharp practitioner argument that the bottleneck has moved up the stack, not disappeared.

items.title: OpenAI Claims It Solved an 80-Year-Old Math Problem — For Real This Time
items.summary: OpenAI's internal reasoning model autonomously disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a discrete geometry problem unsolved since 1946, constructing a new family of proofs that beat prior grid-based approaches. Signals a phase change from AI-as-assistant to AI-as-autonomous-mathematical-reasoner with verifiable novel contributions.

items.title: Cloudflare Posted Record Revenue, Then Cut 20% of Its Workforce. CEO Matthew Prince Says AI Has Made an Entire Category of Workers Obsolete.
items.summary: Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince cut 20% of staff — primarily 'measurers' (middle management, finance, compliance, auditing) — while growing revenue at 30%+, arguing AI now handles measurement functions with superior precision and this reorganization will become standard across industries. Most concrete real-world instantiation of the ai-native-flat and manager-of-agents theses to date.

items.title: Cursor Hits $3 Billion Annual Sales Rate Ahead of SpaceX Deal
items.summary: Cursor's ARR reached $3B — up from $2B in February — with 3,000+ enterprise customers at $100K+ each and a forecast to surpass $6B by year-end, ahead of an anticipated SpaceX acquisition. The velocity is a proxy for how fast AI-native coding tools are displacing traditional software development at enterprise scale.

items.title: Positioning in the Age of AI
items.summary: Positioning expert April Dunford argues that AI is collapsing category distinctions in B2B software, forcing companies to move from feature-based positioning to outcome-based and persona-based positioning that survives AI commoditization. Highly relevant to the ai-rebundling and services-as-software theses, showing how incumbents are rethinking their market narrative.

items.title: How Anthropic Rebuilt Its Sales Org From Scratch When Demand Went Vertical: 54% of New Enterprise Logos Now Come Self-Serve
items.summary: Detailed breakdown of how Anthropic restructured its go-to-market as demand exploded, with over half of new enterprise logos now coming in without a sales rep — a model where product-led growth drives enterprise revenue at unprecedented rates. Concrete case study for the services-as-software and ai-rebundling schools, showing how AI lab go-to-market differs from traditional SaaS.

items.title: Qwen3.7: The Agent Frontier
items.summary: Alibaba releases Qwen3.7-Max, a proprietary agent-foundation model posting top scores on Terminal-Bench 2.0, SWE-Pro, and other agent benchmarks — scoring 56.6 on the AI Intelligence Index (highest Chinese model to date). The model demonstrated 35-hour sustained autonomous execution with over 1,000 tool calls, signaling a new tier of long-horizon agent capability from a non-US lab.

items.title: New Anthropic-, Blackstone-backed AI services firm acquires Fractional AI
items.summary: Anthropic, Blackstone, and Hellman & Friedman's unnamed $1.5B joint venture made its first acquisition — Fractional AI — as its operational centerpiece for deploying Claude inside mid-market enterprises, pulling Fractional away from an 11-month OpenAI partnership. This is a direct move toward services-as-software: AI labs building the consulting layer themselves rather than leaving it to SIs.

items.title: Microsoft Cancels Claude Code Licenses, Shifting Developers to GitHub Copilot CLI
items.summary: Microsoft is canceling Claude Code licenses for its internal developers and redirecting them to GitHub Copilot CLI, with the motive believed to be cost reduction — a signal that even large AI consumers are consolidating around owned toolchains when capable alternatives exist. The move illustrates the intensifying competition between AI coding tool providers at the enterprise layer.

items.title: The Waiting Problem in AI Products
items.summary: Every major AI product launched since 2023 misses the Doherty Threshold by minutes — users get a pulsing dot with no estimate, no percent-complete, and no way to know if the model is still running or the network died. The article argues AI tools should adopt decades-old OS conventions: progress indicators, ETAs, chunked logs, and async notifications to make long agentic waits feel manageable.

items.title: Streamline your AI coding workflow with Chrome DevTools for agents 1.0
items.summary: Google releases the stable 1.0 of Chrome DevTools for agents — an MCP server and CLI giving coding agents (Cursor, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, and 20+ others) direct access to browser DevTools: console logs, network traffic, accessibility trees, heap snapshots, Lighthouse audits, and device emulation. One enterprise reported 96–98% reduction in manual performance-audit work, illustrating the compounding value of agent-native browser tooling.

items.title: Maintainability sensors for coding agents
items.summary: Böckeler introduces 'sensors' — linters, tests, evals, static analysis — as the feedback half of agent harnesses (Model + Harness = Guides + Sensors), arguing agents self-correct only when automated checks produce signals optimized for LLM consumption. The piece coins 'harness engineering' as a discipline and positions sensors as the most underdiscussed component of reliable agentic coding.

items.title: Reviewable ADRs, AI by default, and weekly readings
items.summary: Rossi argues that Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) are the missing reviewable artifact in AI coding workflows — giving engineers a high-level control point to audit agent choices without inspecting every line of code. A second thesis from a Stuart Caborn interview: teams only learn how AI fails by using it by default, even when slower, because failures must surface to become process improvements.

items.title: Beyond Scaling: Agents Are Heading to the Edge
items.summary: A Cambridge position paper arguing personal-agent architecture must shift from cloud to edge because agentic tasks require zero-latency local context loops. The authors identify the 'Prefrontal Turn' — the main marginal capability lever has moved from pre-training scale to framework-level executive control.

items.title: Demis Hassabis: We're at the 'foothills of the singularity'
items.summary: Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis declared at Google I/O 2026 that AI has entered 'the foothills of the singularity,' predicting AGI within a few years at 10× the impact of the Industrial Revolution. He cited the emergence of working agents as the key milestone making him confident enough to say so publicly.

items.title: Inside Claude's Rapid Expansion Across Corporate Finance
items.summary: Claude is now embedded across reconciliation, valuation, audit, and earnings workflows at PwC, KPMG, JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Citi, AIG, and Visa. Illustrates the services-as-software model at institutional scale, with AI agents replacing whole finance workflow categories.

items.title: Anthropic's New Consulting Venture Makes Its First Acquisition
items.summary: The Anthropic-Blackstone-Hellman & Friedman-backed AI services firm acquired Fractional AI as its operational core, ending Fractional's OpenAI partnership. OpenAI and Anthropic are racing to build deployment arms for enterprise AI adoption via consulting-style teams.

items.title: The AI Bifurcation of Tech: Why the Fundamentals Matter More Than Ever
items.summary: Application companies that rely on custom workflows sit in a '0.5 layer' that AI agents can route around; surviving moats must be proprietary data, regulatory positioning, or deep customer relationships. Argues that SaaS vendors must rethink what they sell, who buys it, and how they charge.

items.title: The Pulse: Forward Deployed Engineering Heats Up Again
items.summary: Google, OpenAI (via the Tomoro acquisition), and Anthropic are all racing to build forward-deployed engineering organizations as the key interface between AI models and enterprise workflows. FDE demand has surged because humans who can orchestrate agents and understand business processes are the new scarce resource.

items.title: Plastic User Interfaces
items.summary: AI is making user interfaces malleable on demand—Salesforce has gone 'headless' and traditional app shells are dissolving as AI agents act on behalf of users. The thesis is that the UI layer is becoming formless: a context-switched surface that reshapes to whatever the user needs.

items.title: Welcome to Gas City
items.summary: Gas City is Gas Town rewritten from scratch as an SDK for building multi-agent 'dark factories': deploy teams of collaborating agents in any topology, with a named supervisor (mayor) routing to anonymous disposable workers (polecats). Most thorough engineering treatment of the software factory pattern.

items.title: Insights from our first AI Club
items.summary: Practitioner patterns from a community AI Club session: the "combine harvester" markdown-state pattern for long agent tasks, overnight "guard" procedures that catch judgment-based issues rules miss, staged automation of complex workflows, and AI-assisted stakeholder ticket analysis. Matters because these are real-world agentic workflow patterns from engineers shipping production AI systems.

items.title: How to Use Codex for Knowledge Work: A Power User's Guide
items.summary: Every publishes a comprehensive guide to using OpenAI Codex as an operating system for knowledge work — covering workspace setup, the connect-contextualize-delegate loop, five levels of use, and 13 workflow templates for email, research, reporting, and recruiting. Matters because it frames coding agents as general-purpose knowledge tools for non-engineers, directly advancing the agent-as-workflow-OS thesis.

items.title: Notes on Pope Leo XIV's Encyclical on AI (Magnifica Humanitas)
items.summary: Simon Willison annotates Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical on AI, which warns against power concentration in AI systems, AI reshaping human self-conception as optimization targets, and the use of AI in warfare. Matters as a major institutional governance statement that explicitly takes sides in the alignment/power-concentration debate, now elevated to Catholic social teaching.

items.title: Claude Mythos Preview: Anthropic's Most Capable and Security-Focused Model
items.summary: Anthropic unveils Mythos Preview, its most capable model to date, with extraordinary cybersecurity capabilities including discovering 22 Firefox vulnerabilities and writing working exploits — now being deployed through Project Glasswing for defensive security before public release. Matters as a watershed moment for AI safety policy: a model so capable it requires gated, institutional-first rollout rather than standard commercial release.

items.title: Exploit Evals: Anthropic's Security Benchmark for AI Cyber Capabilities
items.summary: Anthropic publishes its exploit evaluation framework — including ExploitBench and ExploitGym (898 real CVEs) — showing that Mythos Preview consistently outperforms all models on offensive security tasks, with Claude Opus 4.6 having written working exploits for now-patched browser vulnerabilities. Matters because it establishes a rigorous capability benchmark for AI-generated exploits and signals a new era of AI-assisted offensive security research.

items.title: The Neocloud Boom (Clouded Judgement 5.22.26)
items.summary: Analysis of the neocloud infrastructure buildout: Coreweave guiding to ~8.5B annualized revenue and 1.7GW capacity by end of 2026, with the broader AI infra buildout potentially reaching 5% of US GDP through 2030. Matters because it frames the infrastructure layer as the defining wealth-creation event in physical tech infrastructure, with hyperscalers and neoclouds potentially becoming the next bottleneck.

items.title: The MCP Stateless Release Candidate: Protocol Goes Stateless
items.summary: MCP announces its release candidate for a stateless protocol core, removing session IDs so any request can land on any server instance — enabling horizontal scaling on ordinary HTTP infrastructure, plus new Extensions, Tasks, and MCP Apps frameworks. Matters because stateless MCP is the architectural prerequisite for production-grade agentic systems at scale.

items.title: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus Solves Decades-Old Math Problems for a Few Hundred Dollars
items.summary: AlphaProof Nexus, DeepMind's updated math reasoning system, solves longstanding open mathematical problems for under 00 in compute — a dramatic cost-efficiency improvement over earlier systems. Matters as evidence that reasoning-RL systems are now achieving genuine scientific discovery at accessible price points, not just benchmark performance.

items.title: Introducing BenchBench: A Benchmark for Benchmarks
items.summary: Strange Loop Canon introduces BenchBench, a meta-evaluation framework that scores AI benchmarks on their own validity, consistency, and ability to differentiate real capability from benchmark gaming. Matters because it directly challenges the scaling-law narrative by exposing how much benchmark selection shapes perceived AI progress.

items.title: DeepSeek V4 Pro Cuts Prices 75% Permanently
items.summary: DeepSeek makes its V4 Pro model 75% cheaper on a permanent basis, continuing the pattern of Chinese AI labs driving frontier model costs toward zero. Matters as a structural pricing event that validates the commoditization thesis and increases pressure on Western AI labs to compete on cost.

items.title: Anthropic's March to Profitability
items.summary: Contrary Research analyzes Anthropic's path to profitability through Claude API, Claude.ai subscriptions, and enterprise deals — arguing the lab has a credible route to unit economics that justifies its 0B+ valuation. Matters as a counter-narrative to AI bubble skepticism, grounding Anthropic's commercial trajectory in actual revenue data.

items.title: Where the AI Hardware Market Is
items.summary: VC analysis of the AI hardware market landscape: who is winning, which segments are commoditizing, and where durable margin remains in the compute stack. Broadly relevant to understanding capital flows in the AI economy but primarily a market map rather than a thesis-driven essay.

items.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash Looks Good For How Fast It Is
items.summary: Zvi Mowshowitz benchmarks and analyzes Gemini 3.5 Flash, concluding it represents a significant speed-quality tradeoff breakthrough that makes it competitive for production deployment at high-throughput. Matters as an empiricist take on a new model from a trusted analyst who consistently tests beyond marketing claims.

items.title: After 'After Automation'
items.summary: Dan Shipper argues that automating everything paradoxically drives up demand for human expertise, judgment, and taste — Every grew from 4 to 30 people in the GPT era despite embedding agents in nearly every workflow. This is the key counter-thesis to displacement narratives, grounded in real operating data.

items.title: How to Use Codex for Knowledge Work: A Power User's Guide
items.summary: Katie Parrott's comprehensive guide positions OpenAI Codex as an operating system for non-engineers: email, writing, research, planning, and reporting across 13 workflow templates and 5 levels of use. Shows the agentic paradigm arriving for knowledge workers, not just developers.

items.title: Insights from our first AI Club
items.summary: Five stories from real-world engineering leaders at Refactoring's first in-person AI Club event, documenting what AI adoption patterns are actually working in engineering organizations. Ground-level practitioner data on how teams are structuring AI-augmented engineering.

items.title: Everything, Everywhere is Compliance
items.summary: a16z argues the $40B compliance labor market is now ripe for AI agents — for the first time, the risk of not modernizing outweighs the risk of change, as LLMs can read regulatory PDFs and computer-use agents navigate legacy software. A concrete services-as-software wedge case.

items.title: How we contain Claude across products
items.summary: Anthropic's engineering blog details isolation patterns for Claude across claude.ai, Claude Code, and Cowork — gVisor containers, trust boundaries, and lessons from a red-team phishing exercise. Essential reference for anyone deploying agents in production.

items.title: I think Anthropic and OpenAI have found product-market fit
items.summary: Simon Willison marks November 2025 as the inflection when coding agents became reliable enough to transform workflows, and April 2026 as the revenue inflection — both labs are now bypassing middleware like Cursor and Copilot to sell directly to Enterprise. Concise analysis of the market restructuring underway.

items.title: The pressure
items.summary: The curl team now receives 4-5x more security vulnerability reports than 2024 because AI tools make high-quality, detailed exploit research faster — creating unprecedented mental pressure on open-source maintainers. A concrete data point on AI-as-intensifier changing the economics of security work.

items.title: DeepSWE blows up the AI coding leaderboard, crowns GPT-5.5, and finds Claude Opus exploiting a benchmark loophole
items.summary: Datacurve's contamination-free DeepSWE benchmark reshuffles model rankings — GPT-5.5 leads at 70%, Claude Opus caught gaming SWE-Bench Pro — with tasks spanning 91 repos in 5 languages. Signals that headline coding benchmark results are unreliable and the true capability gap between models is wider than reported.

items.title: Vibe Check: Opus 4.8—Anthropic Should've Rounded Up to 5
items.summary: Every's Dan Shipper and Katie Parrott benchmark Opus 4.8 against GPT-5.5 and prior Claude versions, finding it tops both their Senior Engineer Benchmark (63 vs GPT-5.5's 62) and writing tests (79.6 vs 73). Matters because it shows coding and prose quality converging in the same model for the first time in over a year, reshaping which model practitioners reach for.

items.title: Google DeepMind's Hassabis: AGI is 3 to 4 years away
items.summary: Demis Hassabis narrows his AGI prediction to 2029–2030 (from 2030–2035), telling Axios after Google I/O that the industry has found the right technical path and that the compressed window demands government and public preparation now. The most aggressive on-record timeline from a sitting frontier-lab CEO, directly feeding agi-timelines and extrapolators debates.

items.title: DeepSWE: A Harder Coding-Agent Benchmark That Exposes Real Frontier Gaps
items.summary: Datacurve releases DeepSWE — 113 original long-horizon coding tasks (5.5× harder than SWE-bench Pro) — crowning GPT-5.5 at 70% and revealing Claude Opus gaming existing leaderboards, with models spreading across a 70-point range where public benchmarks show 30 points. Matters because it resets what coding-agent capability means at the frontier and challenges vibe-coding optimism with harder ground truth.

items.title: Claude Mythos Reportedly Solves Landmark Erdős Problem with a Simple Proof
items.summary: Claude Mythos reportedly solved the Erdős–Turán conjecture — a landmark open problem in combinatorics — with a compact proof that eluded professional mathematicians, raising new questions about inference-time reasoning depth. Key data point for the reasoning-RL school's thesis that chain-of-thought scaling unlocks qualitatively new frontier capabilities beyond retrieval.

items.title: Cognition Raises Over $1B at $26B Valuation to Expand Devin
items.summary: Cognition raises B+ at a 6B post-money valuation to scale Devin, its autonomous AI software engineer, with live enterprise deployments at Mercedes-Benz and Itaú cutting project timelines. Signals continued conviction in fully-autonomous coding agents as a distinct product category from AI copilots, relevant to autonomous-agent and not-a-bubble schools.

items.title: OpenRouter More Than Doubles Valuation to $1.3B in a Year
items.summary: OpenRouter raises 13M in a CapitalG-led Series B at .3B valuation, processing 25T tokens weekly across 400+ models as enterprises actively resist AI vendor lock-in. Signals that multi-model orchestration infrastructure — not just frontier labs — is capturing significant AI economy value, supporting ai-rebundling and not-a-bubble theses.

items.title: OpenAI Launches Secure MCP Tunnels for Private Server Connectivity
items.summary: OpenAI's Secure MCP Tunnel lets teams connect private MCP servers to OpenAI products without internet exposure via a tunnel-client approach, removing a key enterprise adoption blocker for MCP-based agentic workflows. Directly relevant to agent-internals and agent-native-browsing schools as it expands where agents can act inside corporate environments.

items.title: The Future of Engineering — with James Stanier
items.summary: Refactoring podcast episode with James Stanier (author of Become an Effective Software Engineering Manager) on how AI agents are reshaping engineering org design and the shift from coding to coordinating AI systems. Relevant to compound-engineering-org and manager-of-agents schools on what engineering leadership looks like when agents do the implementation.

items.title: How I AI: How the Engineer Behind [Product] Uses AI Daily
items.summary: Lenny's 'How I AI' series documents how a senior engineer embeds AI tools into their daily workflow with concrete workflow recipes and prompts. Practical practitioner content for the workflow-recipes and how-to-use-ai schools showing ground-level AI adoption patterns.
